## tidydraws

> A tidybayes-inspired data layer for declarative Bayesian visualisation in Python.

`tidydraws` turns MCMC output (ArviZ `DataTree`) into tidy Polars frames that are ready to plot — one `.to_pandas()` away from any ggplot-like backend. It does no plotting itself. Three functions, three spaces:

| Function | Space | Plot archetype |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [`spread_draws()`](docs/examples/spread_draws.qmd) | parameter | density, forest, scatter |
| [`add_epred_draws()`](docs/examples/add_epred_draws.qmd) | prediction | ribbon + line, fit + data |
| [`spread_draws_compare()`](docs/examples/spread_draws_compare.qmd) | comparison | prior vs posterior, intervals |

```{python}
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import polars as pl
import arviz as az
import tidydraws as td
from xarray import DataArray
from lets_plot import *
LetsPlot.setup_html()

np.random.seed(42)
chains, draws, n_groups = 4, 500, 4
n_per, total = 20, 80
group = np.repeat(np.arange(n_groups), n_per)
x = np.random.normal(10, 5, total)
true_int = np.random.normal(0, 0.5, n_groups)
true_slope = np.random.normal(1.0, 0.2, n_groups)
y = np.array([true_int[g] + true_slope[g] * xi + np.random.normal(0, 0.8)
              for g, xi in zip(group, x)])
intercept = np.random.normal(0, 0.5, (chains, draws, n_groups))
beta = np.random.normal(1.0, 0.2, (chains, draws, n_groups))
mu = np.zeros((chains, draws, total))
for i in range(total):
    g = group[i]
    mu[:, :, i] = intercept[:, :, g] + beta[:, :, g] * x[i]
dt = az.from_dict(
    {
        "posterior": {
            "intercept": DataArray(intercept, dims=["chain", "draw", "groups"]),
            "beta": DataArray(beta, dims=["chain", "draw", "groups"]),
        },
        "prior": {
            "beta": DataArray(np.random.normal(0, 0.5, (chains, draws, n_groups)),
                              dims=["chain", "draw", "groups"]),
        },
        "predictions": {"mu": DataArray(mu, dims=["chain", "draw", "obs_ind"])},
        "constant_data": {
            "x": DataArray(x, dims=["obs_ind"]),
            "group": DataArray(group, dims=["obs_ind"]),
            "y": DataArray(y, dims=["obs_ind"]),
        },
    },
    coords={"groups": [f"g{i}" for i in range(n_groups)],
            "obs_ind": list(range(total))},
    dims={"intercept": ["groups"], "beta": ["groups"],
          "x": ["obs_ind"], "group": ["obs_ind"], "y": ["obs_ind"]},
    pred_dims={"mu": ["obs_ind"]},
)

beta_df = td.spread_draws(dt, "beta[groups]")
beta_summary = (
    beta_df.group_by("groups")
    .agg(
        pl.col("beta").quantile(0.055).alias("lower"),
        pl.col("beta").median().alias("median"),
        pl.col("beta").quantile(0.945).alias("upper"),
    )
    .sort("groups")
)
compare = td.spread_draws_compare(dt, "beta[groups]")
pred = td.add_epred_draws(dt, newdata=None, var_name="mu",
                          constant_data_group="constant_data")
pred_summary = (
    pred.group_by("obs_ind", "x", "group")
    .agg(
        pl.col("mu").quantile(0.055).alias("lower"),
        pl.col("mu").median().alias("median"),
        pl.col("mu").quantile(0.945).alias("upper"),
    )
    .sort("x")
)

p_forest = (
    ggplot(beta_summary.to_pandas(), aes("groups", "median"))
    + geom_pointrange(aes(ymin="lower", ymax="upper"), size=0.8)
    + geom_hline(yintercept=0, linetype="dashed", color="#888888")
    + labs(x="group", y="beta", title="Forest plot")
    + theme_minimal()
)
p_density = (
    ggplot(beta_df.to_pandas(), aes("beta", fill="groups"))
    + geom_density(alpha=0.5)
    + labs(x="beta", y="density", fill="group", title="Posterior density")
    + theme_minimal()
)
p_compare = (
    ggplot(compare.to_pandas(), aes("beta", fill="source"))
    + geom_density(alpha=0.5)
    + facet_wrap(facets="groups", ncol=2)
    + labs(x="beta", y="density", fill="source", title="Prior vs posterior")
    + theme_minimal()
)
p_pred = (
    ggplot(pred_summary.to_pandas(), aes("x"))
    + geom_ribbon(aes(ymin="lower", ymax="upper", fill=as_discrete("group")), alpha=0.25)
    + geom_line(aes(y="median", color=as_discrete("group")), size=0.8)
    + labs(x="x", y="mu", color="group", fill="group", title="Predictive fit")
    + theme_minimal()
)

gggrid([p_forest, p_density, p_compare, p_pred], ncol=2)
```


### Install

```bash
uv add tidydraws              # core data layer only
uv add "tidydraws[letsplot]"  # with lets-plot
uv add "tidydraws[plotnine]"  # with plotnine
```

### Why tidydraws?

Plotting MCMC output in Python means manually slicing xarray dimensions, iterating groups, and aligning coordinates — imperative, verbose, error-prone. R's [`tidybayes`](https://github.com/TuringLang/tidybayes) solved this with a data layer that respects parameter space vs prediction space. `tidydraws` brings that to Python on Polars.

| Task | ArviZ (manual) | tidydraws |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Extract `beta[groups]` | `dt.posterior["beta"].values` + reshape | `spread_draws(dt, "beta[groups]")` |
| Compare prior / posterior | concat two arrays by hand | `spread_draws_compare(dt, "beta[groups]")` |
| Join predictions to covariates | manual merge on obs index | `add_epred_draws(dt, var_name="mu")` |
| Subset before plotting | slice after materialising | `.filter(...)` on the returned frame |

::: {.callout-note}
`tidydraws` is plotting-backend-agnostic: it returns Polars `DataFrame`s, and
`.to_pandas()` bridges to lets-plot, plotnine, or any library that takes
pandas. The examples use [lets-plot](https://lets-plot.org/); see the
[`spread_draws()`](docs/examples/spread_draws.qmd) page for the same plot in
plotnine.
:::

---

*Inspired by [tidybayes](https://github.com/TuringLang/tidybayes) for R.*
